data.title: REET: The Real Estate Sector Remains Unexciting, Non-U.S. REITs Included
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the iShares Global REIT ETF (REET) as an investment option at its current market price. This ETF has an objective to "track the investment results of an index composed of global real estate equities in developed and emerging markets". It holds mostly US-based REITs, but roughly 30% of its holdings are located in non-US jurisdictions.

It has been a long time since I wrote about REET, and even longer since I was bullish on it! My last review was over five years ago and I had a neutral view on the fund. This has turned out to be the right call since then because despite REET offering a positive return, it has sharply lagged the broader market:

With all the time that has passed, I figured it was overdue for another look at REET. After all, sector plays that have been out of favor for a while (which Real Estate has) can often be sources of "alpha" when timed correctly.

However, I don't see that happening as we look to enter 2026. Real Estate - whether through REET or other funds - has been a laggard for good reasons. There are some positives, yes, but I think the headwinds outweigh some of this potential opportunity and lead me to think "hold" remains appropriate. I will explain why in detail below.

A Recap Of 2025 Performance

As mentioned in the opening paragraph, REET has given investors some gains but the results have been less than impressive when we consider how the broader market has rallied. Yet, that is over a 5-year period, so it is important to consider how Real Estate has been doing more recently. Unfortunately, the results may even be worse by comparison. While gold, EM stocks, and the S&P 500 have rallied in 2025, REITs have barely kept up with inflation:

What we are seeing here is that Real Estate has been a bit of a downer this year and that has compounded the under-performance we have seen longer term. Could this reverse going forward and allow investors to capitalize in 2026? Potentially - but I'm not going to gamble on it. I think it is important to understand that momentum is not on this sector's side (or on REET's side) and that is not a trend that is favorable for me.

What Is The Good News?

I will dive into REET by first reminding readers I am not a "bear" on this fund. I noted I am not thrilled with the chance of "alpha" with this option and don't see out-performance as likely. That is why I have some caution - but I do see some chance for upside. Part of the reason has to do with the fund's strong allocation to retail REITs. At almost one-fifth total exposure, how the global retail space is doing is of particular importance to this ETF's chances:

This may seem like an odd place to be given the challenges retail has faced since the pandemic. But despite the doom and gloom of some critics, retail has actually been faring quite well. Yes, e-commerce continues to grab a larger share of the price, but brick and mortar retail is still very much alive and well. Further, the pandemic helped weed out some under-performing companies and retail spaces which leaves us with a decent backdrop today (think the "strong survive").

Beyond those points we should also acknowledge that retail sales have been rising at a steady clip. With some bumps along the way, total sales volume is up strong year-over-year in the past five years and that supports investors in the retail REIT space as those underlying retailers collect this rising revenue:

What I see here is a healthy backdrop for the retail space. As long as the dollars are flowing to retailers, they make good tenants, and that can make investors in retail REITs happy by extension. I therefore view this heavy allocation for REET as a positive and use this attribute as a fundamental reason for not being bearish on the fund.

Global Office Leasing Is Still Historically Low

Now I want to talk about some of the challenges. One in particular that has me concerned is how the office space dynamic continues to face pressures from both rising layoff announcements to the work-from-home culture. This has kept the pressure on office REITs as office space continues to see slack demand due to those headwinds. Yes, prime space continues to command a premium, but most office space is not prime! And we can see that total volume of leased corporate office space remains well below pre-Covid levels:

Going forward I don't anticipate a major shift in this backdrop and that will pressure multi-sector funds such as REET. A reason why I don't expect much of a change is that hiring has slowed, notably in the US. While we are far from an employment "crisis", the slowing of hiring is going to prevent much of a surge in office space leasing in the year ahead:

The simple fact is I don't see a catalyst for a sudden surge in office space demand. I'm not trying to be alarmist. Again, I don't see this sub-sector as a "sell" in a similar way I don't see Real Estate as a "sell". But I don't love the picture either. I want to invest in areas that are growing or I believe will be growing soon. That is not what I see in the office REIT realm and I therefore see this sub-sector counter-balancing some of the positives for Real Estate funds as a whole.

REET Has Gotten Less Global

Another factor to keep in mind is how REET is broken down by country. One of the attributes that got me interested in this fund in the first place was its non-US exposure. It is a way to diversify a US-centric portfolio (which I have) and could help round out one's holdings. However, it is worth noting that over time REET has gotten less "global". Back in 2020 it had 66% of its holdings in the US jurisdiction. Today, that figure has risen to over 70%:

this is not inherently "good" or "bad" but it is important to understand. This is particularly true since iShares markets REET as a "global" ETF. And I'm not saying they are wrong because it is. Fair play to them because almost 30% of the underlying securities are firms based in other countries.

But the challenge I have is this may not be diverse enough. The correlation between REET and other US-only Real Estate funds is quite clear. The performance of this one and a variety of US ETFs over the past year is shown below:

My takeaway from this is that REET is not going to really balance out US-focused REITs if one already has that exposure. Again, that doesn't make REET an nonviable option - it could certainly work well in one's portfolio for the right reason.

But my point is that if one already owns one or more of the funds listed in the above graphic ((VNQ), (SCHH), (IYR)), then it probably won't do much to buy into REET. Despite having a global tilt, it is too US heavy to really offer much diversification for those mentioned Real Estate ETFs. This should be kept in mind for anyone considering starting a position in any of these funds.

Income Plays Are In-Demand

My final point will help explain why I even considered REET in this macro-environment and why I will keep it on my radar in case something fundamental changes. If the Real Estate market looks set to improve and/or this fund sees an unjustifiable sell-off, I may very well be interested in initiating a position even though I don't at this exact moment. Part of the reason is that income plays of all stripes are getting more compelling as the Federal Reserve continues to lower its benchmark interest rate.

And there are valid reasons for this. The most obvious is that when the yield on treasuries goes down, income-oriented investors may need to look elsewhere to generate a higher revenue stream. This includes corporate bonds, muni bonds, dividend paying stocks, and, of course, REITs. And history often favors those who do this. When interest rates are in a narrow and moderate range, REITs have tended to out-perform equities on an annual basis:

The caveat is this is no guarantee. The past few years can demonstrate that! But it does show that, longer term, REITs tend to do quite well when rates have been declining but are not at ultra-lows. (note: when rates are "too low" that can often be the result of a weak economy and REITs don't tend to perform well in that backdrop).

The reason I am highlighting this is there is plenty of merit to considering REITs in this environment and while I don't see a buy case just yet, that could change. So REET - and other funds like it - will remain top-of-mind for me as we progress towards the new year.

Bottom-Line

REET has been under-performing for a while now and I don't see a compelling case in the near term. True, it has pumped out a reasonable gain and strong retail sales and historical performance in a lower rate environment are reasons to be bullish. But weak momentum and modest global office leasing figures are balancing out some of those positives for me. As a result, I am keeping the "hold" rating in place for this fund going forward. I would caution my followers to approach any new positions very selectively at this time.

data.title: Cameco Q3: $80B Deal, Record Highs... And A Reality Check
data.description:
A few days after having published “Cameco: Leading the Western Nuclear Renaissance”, the story took a breakneck speed.

On October 28 2025, Cameco (CCJ), and Brookfield announced, along with the US government, an $80B strategic agreement in order to develop a new generation of Westinghouse nuclear reactors.

The announcement unleashed a wave of optimism that led to asset to increase 21% in just one day, reaching a new all– time maximum.

However, the initial enthusiasm yielded upon the results of the third quarter that showed a fall in income and a slightly positive benefit.

The market took the chance to take profits, and the stock retreated part of the rally, though still operating way above the levels at the beginning of the year.

Even then, I still see Cameco as a central piece in the new western energy architecture.

Its business model, increasingly more integrated with Westinghouse and Brookfield, grants it a unique position within a reindustrialization context and an energy demand boosted by AI.

But after the strong revaluation and numbers expansion, I adjust my Strong Buy stance to Buy: the structural story remains, but the immediate entry point is less attractive.

Q3 2025 Results

After analyzing the results of Q3 2025, I consider that while it was a weaker quarter in revenues, CCJ showed a remarkable ability to sustain margins and maintain a very comfortable financial position.

In total, revenues were CA$614 MM, a decrease of 15% YoY, almost entirely explained by deliveries of uranium that customers themselves decided to move towards the end of the year. That is, there was no structural drop in demand, but a change of timing.

Net earnings was virtually zero, around CA$0.16 MM, compared to CA$7.4 MM in the same period of 2024. Even so, the adjusted EPS rose from CA$0.06 to CA$0.07, a small improvement but, in my view, consistent with an operation that managed to absorb the temporary drop in volumes quite well.

The company itself explained the context clearly in its conference call:

Lower realized sales volumes in uranium and fuel services impacted revenue this quarter, as we continue to align deliveries with customer timing and production optimization.

The operating flow remained stable, supported by better realized prices and a greater contribution from the Fuel Services segment, which once again acted as a buffer for the movements of the mining business.

The company ended the quarter with CA$779MM in cash and a completely available credit line for CA$1B, maintaining a net liquidity position that grants it flexibility in order to sustain investments or future acquisitions.

Cameco closed the quarter with CA$716 MM in cash and an available credit line of CA$1 B, a liquidity position that gives it room to act even if the market is going through less favorable periods.

On the operational front, the company followed a deliberately cautious approach. Combined production at McArthur River and Key Lake was again between 14 and 15 Mlb (basis 100%), below the originally planned 18 Mlb.

Cameco is prioritizing long-term stability over immediate volume, and in my view, that discipline makes sense in a uranium market where supply remains tight.

The agreement that redefines its position

Although the numbers are very important when it comes to seeing a company’s performance and its future projections, for me the most relevant of the quarter was the strategic agreement announced by Cameco, Brookfield and the US government.

This is a pact that seeks to accelerate the deployment of Westinghouse’s AP1000 reactors and could mobilize more than $80B in future investments. For Cameco, this is not just a commercial opportunity, but a profound change in its role within the western nuclear chain.

Tim Gitzel, CEO of Cameco, put it clearly in the statement of 28 October 2025:

We expect that the new build commitments from the US will bolster broader confidence in the durable growth profile for nuclear power, and support increased demand for Westinghouse’s and Cameco’s products, services and technologies.

The agreement states that the US government could receive up to 20% of Westinghouse’s distributions once $17.5 B is exceeded, and would also have the possibility of acquiring an equivalent stake in a future IPO.

However, on November 5, Cameco went out to clarify the actual scope of the pact. As Gitzel remarked that day,

the participation interest by the U.S. government is only focused on the Westinghouse business.

This clarification closed the confusion that had arisen in the media regarding a possible interference with mining operations, something that the company explicitly ruled out.

For me, the real shift is the strategic scale behind the agreement. Cameco aligns with an American energy agenda that needs new nuclear capacity to sustain electrification, energy security and the growing demand for AI infrastructure and data centers. This places the company in a structural position, with a much deeper role than that of a simple miner or fuel manufacturer.

Valuation, growth and profitability

If we look at the photo of the last twelve months, the numbers are strong.

Revenues grew 34.7% YoY, EBITDA rose 62.5%, EBIT advanced 74.4%, and diluted EPS more than doubled (+106% YoY). To me, this combination reflects a very balanced mix of favorable pricing, cost discipline and an operation that became more efficient over time.

Margins also remained high. The EBITDA margin was 26.8%, the operating reached 16.8% and the net closed at 14.9%.

The company generated a FCF equivalent to 20.4% of sales, while the ROA reached 5.6% and the ROE at 8.2%. CapEx/S stood at 7.2%, which in my opinion remains a moderate and reasonable level that suggests a prudent strategy to sustain capacity without over-investing.

The least favourable point is valuation. When I analyze the metrics in detail, I notice that Cameco trades with multiples that already incorporate an optimistic scenario, as I had already mentioned in my previous article.

Whereas the EV/EBITDA FWD is 31x, the P/E FWD is around 99x, the EV/S is at 17x, and the P/B reaches 8.4x, clearly above the sector medians.

In other words, I still see the same as last month, and that is that the market already recognizes its strategic position, paying a significant premium for it, which for me limits the potential for an immediate revaluation.

Risks

The risks are not new, but they remain relevant.

Operationally, I must point out that the transition to new production areas in McArthur River and adjustments at Key Lake require technical precision, so any deviation could have temporary impacts on volumes or costs.

In addition, I must also mention that projects associated with Westinghouse move on long timelines and staggered milestones, which always opens the door to delays or reschedulings.

In financial terms, valuation remains high, leaving CCJ with little margin for error. If uranium prices moderate or the global nuclear momentum moves more slowly than is currently being discounted, for me multiples could be adjusted rapidly.

Conclusion

My reading remains the same as the previous month, and for me Cameco remains the backbone of the new western nuclear cycle.

While Q3 did not stand out for its revenue, it did make it clear that the company can defend margins and sustain a robust operation even in a difficult quarter. The agreement with the US adds a structural layer of growth which, in my view, changes its competitive profile for the next decade.

After the announcement rally and subsequent correction, the stock is trading at somewhat more reasonable levels, although without the discount it had months ago. Therefore, I reduce my recommendation from Strong Buy to Buy.

For me, Cameco remains a structurally solid, company financially disciplined and strategically indispensable, although its current valuation undoubtedly reflects much of the future that the market is already anticipating.

data.title: Conagra Brands: Income And Stability In A Turbulent Market
data.description:
Conagra Brands (CAG) may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors, seeking defensive approach. This could help to withstand market turbulence, especially highlighting that the volatility has returned. This consumer packaged goods company specializes in the space of manufacturing and marketing food products. The company covers three different segments of service provision, including retail, commercial customers, and foodservice.

The stock is down by roughly 37% over the past 12 months, representing significant market underperformance. CAG currently trades at a forward P/E of approximately 10, indicating a multi-year low valuation, and a 60% discount versus the sector median of 16x earnings multiple. I think the downward trend appears overdone, and I argue the stock seems to be well-positioned to rebound from these levels.

I think Conagra Brands could be an interesting diversification alternative, presenting a defensive approach. Moreover, the forward dividend yield of 8% appears compelling, and may seem as an attractive bonus to an undervalued stock. Although the company experiences top and bottom line headwinds, I think the major concerns may have already been priced into the stock price. Now that the volatility has returned to the broader markets, such stable and predictable businesses may experience demand.

I rate Conagra Brands a Buy with a $20 price target over the next year. This represents a 14% upside possibility without dividends, indicating broader market outperformance.

FQ1 2026: Double-Beat

On October 1, the company delivered FQ1 2026 results. Although the consumer staples name posted a double-beat quarter, it actually experienced a decline in both top and bottom line. Concerns around CAG's future outlook remain, yet the market rewarded the stock with a 5% upside on the earnings day, supporting my thesis that major headwinds may already be priced in the stock price.

Conagra Brands delivered $2.63 billion in revenue, nearly a 6% drop on a year-over-year basis, but $15 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $0.39 in diluted EPS, a decline versus $0.53 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, yet $0.06 above analysts' estimates.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted that in terms of organic net sales, CAG generated $2.61 billion, indicating roughly flat performance over the past 12 months. This slightly improves the company's outlook, arguing that top-line contraction may not be accelerating. Nevertheless, I think investors should monitor whether the performance remains stable.

Management underlined 24.4% of adjusted gross margin, and 11.8% of adjusted operating margin, a 153 bps and 244 bps decline, respectively, clouding the bullish narrative, and indicating continuation of margin erosion. I would also like to emphasize that there remain obvious reasons justifying the sell-off and suppressed valuation, but with improving consumer spending driven by the Fed's easing cycle, and forward-looking markets, CAG may rebound from these levels.

The segment of grocery and snacks delivered $1.08 billion in net sales, and experienced a 1% decline in terms of organic net sales over the past 12 months. The category of refrigerated and frozen generated roughly $1.08 billion in net sales, as well, yet remained flat on a year-over-year basis. Although this may appear concerning, the US consumer spending remains clouded, and that impacts CAG's performance. On the other hand, it shows resilience, stability, and business maturity, which may argue for a higher premium.

The international business experienced a 3.5% decline on a year-over-year basis and 5% drop in volume. Although it may indicate structural issues for the company, international sales account for less than 10% of the total revenue in the quarter. This remains an area to monitor for investors, but could also show that management focuses on strengthening its moat in the North America market, rather than pursuing sales internationally. The foodservice business generated $265 million in net sales and remained flat on a year-over-year basis.

Based on the quarterly performance, it seems to be more macroeconomic-related headwinds tied to weaker consumer spending than a structural shift in consumer preferences or loss of market share. This is also supported by the following slide, representing 44% of Conagra's portfolio holding or gaining volume share on a year-over-year basis.

Management highlighted a few key headwinds in the near term. The company anticipates a continuation of the core inflation, which could result in further pressure on margins. I also believe that inflation in commodities, such as corn, meat, and oil, could impact the overall performance and partially cloud the turnaround story in the near term. Tariffs remain another significant concern, and that could heavily contribute to the company's outlook. Furthermore, consumer sentiment remains weak, although I believe in the short term the Fed's easing cycle could act as a meaningful tailwind.

I would also like to emphasize that the company experienced margin deterioration across all categories, except international. The segment of refrigerated and frozen experienced the most severe decline, underscoring a 28% drop on a year-over-year basis in terms of adjusted operating profit margin. If the trend continues further, there is a possibility that the market may not be willing to pay a premium for the company's stock.

Management has also guided FY2026. CAG anticipates flat top-line performance on a year-over-year basis, approximately 11.25% in adjusted operating margin at the midpoint, and roughly $1.78 in diluted EPS. This indicates top and bottom-line headwinds, but I don't think CAG faces structural risks. With improved consumer spending, I would anticipate resumed revenue growth. Time will tell, but management guides for business stability, yet also stagnation, which, given economic uncertainties and slowdown in consumer spending, could appear reasonable.

Conagra Brands Remains Undervalued

Conagra Brands trades at a forward P/E of approximately 10, suggesting significant undervaluation versus peers at a 16x earnings multiple. The company is also trading at nearly 2.3x discount to the average company in the S&P500 at a 23x earnings multiple. The chart below reveals a multi-year low valuation for the consumer staples name, and historically, it has rebounded from these levels.

The forward Price/Sales ratio of 0.74 suggests a 31% discount versus the sector median of 1.08. This indicates that the market has not priced in the future revenue growth, and if CAG surprises with better-than-anticipated results, there is room for multiple expansion.

On the downside, CAG appears to be heavily leveraged, and that contributes to the bearish narrative. The consumer staples company has $8.28 billion in total obligations, which results in a significant financial burden for the company. Over the past 12 months, CAG paid nearly $409 million in interest expense to service its debt. On the other hand, if the Fed continues the funding rate easing cycle, the company may be able to refinance its obligations on more favorable conditions. This could lead to lower interest expenses and support bottom-line growth.

Over the past 12 months, CAG experienced a 4% revenue decline, which validates headwinds and indicates significant underperformance versus peers at 3% top-line growth. However, given the fact that the stock is down by 37% over the past 12 months, I argue that the majority of the concerns may have already been priced into the stock price.

In terms of profitability metrics, Conagra Brands presents a mixed picture. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 26% reflects approximately a 29% disadvantage versus peers at 36%. However, the net income margin (TTM) of 7.42% suggests a 65% outperformance versus peers at 4.49%. This argues that despite the current headwinds tied to tariffs and macroeconomic uncertainty, CAG remains highly profitable, and that deserves a premium. The company generated $1.54 billion in cash from operations (TTM), implying a 91% advantage versus the sector median of $808 million.

To conclude, CAG experiences top and bottom line headwinds, which may cloud the future outlook in the near term. However, based on the analysis and quarterly performance, it seems that the business managed to prevent revenue deceleration, which also highlights resilience, a strong moat, a solid market position, and stability. The company also operates at market-like margins, which argues for a closer-to-industry earnings multiple. At the current levels, the stock remains significantly undervalued.

Conagra Brands also offers dividend income. The forward yield of more than 8% may appear compelling, and I argue that it could be a significant bonus to an undervalued consumer staples name. This also represents a 2.6x higher yield than the sector median of 3%. To highlight, CAG hasn't raised its dividends since 2023, and that may suggest that management is likely to focus on paying the debt, rather than proceeding with dividend growth.

The cash dividend payout ratio (TTM) of 59% suggests that investors shouldn't worry too much about the continued shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach. Although this indicates a 12% premium versus peers at 52%, the company has consecutively paid dividends for 36 years, and I think the trend is likely to continue further.

Wall Street forecasts $1.87 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2027. This would represent a resumed bottom-line growth, suggesting a 6% increase versus the fiscal FY2026 estimate of $1.76. Based on my analysis and the Fed's easing cycle, I think consumer spending may accelerate, which could establish a more favorable environment for CAG. The company remains well-positioned in the market, although it stagnates. Conagra Brands has well-recognizable brands, such as Slim Jim, Birds Eye, and Hunt's, which may drive the future revenue growth further and sustain its dominant position.

I also believe investor confidence may slightly improve, following a more positive outlook due to a lower interest rate environment. Therefore, if we apply an 11x earnings multiple to the fiscal FY2027 EPS estimate of $1.87, we arrive at my price target of $20. Although this would indicate a 10% higher premium than it currently is, it would still signal more than 45% undervaluation versus peers. In addition to this, I argue that CAG deserves a higher earnings multiple due to stable and resilient revenue streams and an ongoing cost-saving and business optimization strategy. Conagra Brands also offers a defensive approach, and the demand for consumer staples may arise if the market becomes more volatile.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Conagra Brands that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in the short term.

The company remains heavily dependent on the macroeconomic outlook and consumer spending. In case the economy enters a recession or the Fed decides to pause the easing cycle, sentiment may shift, and the stock price may decline. Although CAG remains a consumer staples name, it is likely that the majority of the stock market may correct in the short term.

Conagra Brands experiences weak demand in the international markets. Although this remains an insignificant part of the company's total revenue, this partially clouds the company's outlook. Investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the business if it becomes less diversified and loses market share to competition.

CAG profitability metrics are highly dependent on rising input costs. If inflation rises in commodity prices, such as corn, oil, and meat, this could negatively impact business performance and result in lower bottom-line results. This could pressure the stock, and CAG may continue trading at a discount.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, Conagra Brands appears to be well-positioned to maintain its market-leading position in the operating industry.

Conclusion

I think Conagra Brands may appear to be a compelling opportunity to consider for long-term investors seeking a defensive approach, which could help withstand market turbulence. Its consumer staples nature may appear appealing, considering the broader markets have rallied since the April lows without experiencing a pullback. If the markets shift to a risk-off environment, CAG, alongside other names, may experience demand for its stock.

The company remains facing top and bottom line headwinds, which may cloud the bullish outlook and turnaround play. However, I think these issues are not structural, but rather tied to macroeconomic headwinds and a weak US consumer. If the Fed continues the easing cycle, this could boost the sentiment, and top and bottom-line growth may resume.

Conagra Brands also appears to be trading at a multi-year low and offers dividend income, which makes it a compelling opportunity for income-seeking investors. That's why I think Conagra Brands is a Buy with a $20 price target, indicating a 14% upside possibility without dividends. 17 Wall Street analysts project a $20.34 12-month price target, which is roughly in line with my expectations.

data.title: Gladstone Capital: The BDC Is Back On The Growth Track, It's A Buy
data.description:
Gladstone Capital (GLAD) is one of the few BDCs in my portfolio that is underwater on a total return basis. For example, since my previous article that was published in September, 2025, GLAD has declined by ~18%. Granted, the entire BDC sector (BIZD) is down as well, but certainly not to the same extent at GLAD:

Also, it is important to note that relatively recently GLAD announced a dividend cut, reducing its monthly base distribution amount by 9%.

However, today (November 18) GLAD's share price is up by almost 5% - driven by the positive earnings report - Q4, 2025 (covering period from July to September, 2025). I have taken a deeper look into the key data points and have to say that I still remain bullish on this case.

In my view, it is really a matter of time (couple of quarters max) before GLAD registers a significant recovery that would be fueled by accretive equity monetizations. In the meantime, while we wait for the equity exits to take place, we can safely collect the adjusted dividend, which now still provides fairly decent yield of 9.6%.

Let me give you more details here.

Thesis review

I think that we have to first start with the dividend cut and why it happened. There were couple of reasons that motivated the Management to take this step. Namely, it was really a combination of ~100% base dividend coverage going into Q4 (i.e., no margin of safety to absorb NII pressure), lowered base rates by the Fed and, importantly, a huge portfolio turnover, which basically resulted in an immediate portfolio yield convergence to market-level yields (e.g., over the TTM period, GLAD has turned over ~42% of the portfolio assets).

So, given these dynamics and the expectation that going forward there will very likely be more rate cuts announced, the Management decided to make a minor adjustment to the distribution amount in order to (and this is important) preserve the balance sheet. Theoretically, GLAD could easily tap into its balance sheet to keep the dividend alive for many future quarters without exceeding the sector average debt to equity of 1.19x. Currently, GLAD has a debt to equity(NAV) of 0.82x, which is one of the lowest levels in the BDC space. However, what this would be just a short-term fix at the expense of a) financial resiliency and b) opportunistic balance sheet growth option.

I really liked how GLAD's President - Robert Marcotte - explained this in the Q4 earnings conference call:

I think the challenge is 100 basis point decline is going to pressure us as well as everyone else and how do we absorb that? Well, we'll absorb it through -- if you note in our financials, we paid an awful lot of commitment fees on our line of credit that we didn't use. So we probably are going to reduce that by virtue of what we've done in the restructuring of the business. We also had a very light quarter from a fee load perspective. I expect those fees to increase. And the combination of those as well as some of the dividend reduction, I think it puts us in a much more healthy position to maintain the current dividend. I don't feel that we're under any particular pressure at this point. It was just really more of setting expectations going into 2026, given the rates are already beginning to decline.

Having said that, I want to make it clear that experiencing a dividend cut was not my base case. My thesis was that GLAD would be able to crystalize its embedded equity value, which typically comes with outsized earnings (gains) that can both reduce leverage even further and accommodate base and enticing supplemental distributions. A great example of this was mid-2024, when on top of monthly distributions, GAIN positively surprised its investors by announcing $0.40 per share of a supplemental dividend that was fueled by a successful equity sale.

Why this kind of event has not happened so far this year? Well, because of initially stagnant M&A markets, where we had the activity levels quite depressed until really post Q2. Now, in BDC Q3 report, we can see a significant uptick in transaction volumes, which makes sense given the lower base rates and reduced tariff uncertainty.

GLAD has not been an exception on this front. The fundings in Q4, 2025 came in at $126.6 million, which nicely exceed $23.5 million of repayment. The net originations of $103.1 million is really a huge figure for GLAD since the usual annual portfolio growth has been at around $50 million. Plus, in the context of Q3, 2025 asset size of ~$750 million, having this kind of a net funding figure is indeed significant.

What this growth implies is that going forward, we should see a solid boost to GLAD's NII per share; similar as in Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) case, where the BDC increased its leverage (portfolio size) from 0.7x to 0.9x, which helped achieve ~7.5% of a NII per share growth despite pressures from lower base rates and some tiny non-accruals (all going from Q3 to Q4, 2025).

Actually, if we look at the Q4 data points, we will see that things have started to happen on the ground already now. The NII per share increased to $0.52 (a 4% quarter-to-quarter move), which could be easily deemed as a positive exception as most of the BDCs have registered declining NII per share results. Here it is again important to note that GLAD was able to capture this growth despite 30 basis points of a portfolio yield compression (due to lower base rates and spread compression). The fact that the average earnings asset base increased by ~16.2% not only offset the pressures, but also fueled growth. It just speaks about the inherent power of growing the asset base.

And here let me emphasize the following:

  • Based on the updated dividend policy, GLAD can now reinvest ~15% of its quarterly NII back in the business (alternatively, use that to combat future NII headwinds).
  • Since the balance sheet is still significantly overcapitalized and there is a strong momentum both in the overall M&A market and in GLAD's organization (think of record funding quarter), it would be reasonable to expect some further portfolio growth, which should accommodate NII per share expansion.

The bottom line

Seeing the recent developments for GLAD, I can fully understand why the market has reacted positively to the earnings report.

In essence, GLAD has clearly strengthened its position as sustainable dividend income BDC - due to a combination of a more balanced dividend and, importantly, loan investment base expansion, which directly stimulates NII per share growth.

Why is that important? First, it simply makes the current income streams less risky and more suitable for durable income investors. Second, it de-risks the process of GLAD investors waiting for parts of its ~10% equity portfolio (based on total investment amount) to be monetized as the M&A markets continue to rebound.

In my opinion, GLAD announcing sizeable equity sales is what will be necessary for the BDC to neutralize the current ~8% discount to NAV.

Finally, I have decided to keep GLAD in my equity-focused BDC bucket together with Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) and Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS) (with GLAD being the smallest one).

data.title: Bitmine: Ethereum's Boldest Bet Is Here
data.description:
Investment Thesis

BitMine Immersion Technologies’ (BMNR) investment thesis rests on an audacious tightrope of continuously raising equity with the sole intention of obtaining the hard-pressed yet ambitious goal of 5% of the Ethereum supply. Currently sitting on this tightrope with its balance sheet boasting a whopping 3.6 million ETH, BitMine stands out not only as an Ethereum-proxy company but as an investment vehicle on the Ethereum agenda.

Nevertheless, its tale extends well beyond that. As ETH/BTC marginally increases with Ethereum’s prevalence as the blockchain of choice for the tokenization of various assets and virtual financial transactions, BitMine finds itself on the same pedestal that MicroStrategy (MSTR) occupied on Bitcoin’s pedestal, the only form of significant growth that Bitcoin currently receives.

While dilution risk looms, opportunistic investors can view sharp pullbacks as speculative entry points, betting on ETH upside and BMNR's ability to convert narrative momentum into shareholder value. Ideal for crypto-aligned portfolios seeking asymmetric return potential.

Why Crypto’s Next Move Hinges on Fragile Liquidity and Mixed Macro Signals

Looking at the macro environment that precedes the beginning of the new year of 2026, it’s a bit of a mixed bag in the world of cryptocurrency. On the positive side of things, the Fed has halted its rate hikes on interest rates with the expectation that more favorable conditions may come about. However, the fact that inflation still exists causes the markets to tread carefully.

On the other hand, the markets just recently experienced the effects of forceful selling within the world of cryptocurrency that left its markets highly vulnerable to disruption. Market makers have pulled back significantly due to this fact. This makes the markets behave more erratically than expected due to the lack of liquidity.

This implies that even small trading activity could cause the markets to move more than usual. This could indeed result in sharp rallies based on the effects of positive news events that could happen. But it also means downside moves can get exaggerated quickly.

BitMine’s $24B Ethereum Gambit Chases MicroStrategy Glory

What started out as a mining corporation today serves the purpose of an ETH holding company. BitMine owns a total of 3.56 million ETH, 192 BTC, and $607 million of funds. The stated mission of the company is the attainment of the so-called “Alchemy of 5%” with the ETH supply. This corresponds with around 6 million ETH purchases with an extra price tag of around $7-8 billion.

This isn’t just a hypothetical mission, the company’s filings talk about the valuation of the equity issuance program at the price of $24.5 billion. This is the point of practical application. The share base has grown exponentially with the increments of ETH share price at the grace of dilution. The company’s latest NAV filings don’t even reflect the share price of NAV.

But there’s a precedent with the model too. MicroStrategy took on the role of the Bitcoin proxy through its leverage of debt and equity. They currently hold 650,000 BTC and operate very close to their Money NAV. BitMine wants the same with ETH. What’s different? Execution. MicroStrategy had a cash-flowing business with software. BitMine is mining out of its past with its speculation on ETH’s future. All action on the price climb.

The Tom Lee Variable: Catalyst or Risk of Story?

As the Chairman of BitMine, Tom Lee’s role cannot only be considered strategic but more so ideological. Lee expects ETH at the price of $7,000 at the end of the year and anticipates the top of the market occurring within the next 12-36 months. That will give BitMine sufficient time to accumulate capital. His reputation comes with the group of fans of the bullish predictions of Fundstrat. However, this could prove counterproductive should the ETH price fail.

But I must say Lee’s got company. BitMine’s investors include Galaxy Digital, Kraken , Pantera, Founders’ Fund, and ARK’s Cathie Wood. But with this sort of highly correlated stock instrument, the effects of sentiment flip-flop can be brutal. BMNR’s currently down about 50% from the October highs. Lack of NAV disclosure and the enormous share issuance program give bears more than an initiation point.

Nevertheless, the bullish argument rests on cognitive biases as well. Should ETH experience a reflexive move owing to various tokenization-related positives along with rate cuts and macro trends of risk-on shifts, BitMine’s potential seems greatly multiplied. But the question arises whether investors could get diluted prior to the rewards.

Lastly, Crypto sentiment has turned to Extreme Fear, with the CMC Fear and Greed Index plunging to 15, the lowest reading of the year. This level, last seen in March 2025, reflects a sharp deterioration in investor confidence, likely exacerbated by recent liquidity collapses and October’s marketwide deleveraging. Historically, such sentiment troughs have preceded strong rebounds, making the current environment a potential accumulation zone for high-beta assets like BMNR, especially for investors who believe ETH fundamentals remain intact despite macro-driven volatility.

NAV, Premium Volatility: Decoding the Risk-Reward Exchange

BitMine offers a slight discount relative to its own announced NAV of approximately 0.99x mNAV. This discount of nearly a quarter-off price marks the value of BitMine relative to MicroStrategy's 1.24x mNAV. And the reason this discount should persist? Because execution risk remains very high.

To buy ETH with an inflow of new shareholders (via new share distribution), BitMine must issue more of its shares. And thus the paradox emerges because BitMine requires an expensive equity price to operate but its equity price trades at a discount because of fear of execution. Indeed, the dilution problem is genuine.

However, BitMine on the medium term horizon (6-18 months) could prove an attractive but very volatile Ethereum call option. A new ETH price system that makes tokenization the new standard on Wall Street could cause BMNR’s price to take-off if the company starts staking and engaging with the Ethereum community.

But investors must stay cautious. If the volatility persists or if there’s a shift in the Ethereum dominance due to the growing pace of cheaper Layer 1 blockchains, its structure becomes speculative if not strategically positioned beforehand. This investment cannot remain a buy-and-forget option.

Takeaway

BMNR is a speculation vehicle on Ethereum with institutional backing and the kind of token exposure that few possess. The company’s stock hasn’t yet bottomed out due to the NAV buffer that prevents the stock price from plummeting further, on the other hand, its strategy of investing only 5% of its capital in ETH could prove extremely lucrative if the macro trends turn favorable.

data.title: Alpha Metallurgical Resources: Met Coal Blues Continue, $100 A Share Likely Again - Sell
data.description:
Introduction

Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR) is one of the largest metallurgical coal producers in the United States, focused on high-quality met coal for the global steel industry (three-quarters of production are exported) out of Central Appalachia.

Born from the ashes of the 2015-2016 coal apocalypse, the company emerged from the Chapter 11 bankruptcy of Alpha Natural Resources and Contura Energy. The pivotal 2021 merger between Contura and the met-coal assets of bankrupt Blackjewel (formerly Alpha Natural) created today's AMR—a phoenix that rose and became a met-coal juggernaut.

AMR raised awareness, especially through its massive buybacks when coal prices went through the roof a few years ago (share count dropped by some 30%). Its stock multi-bagged, reaching $400, but a painful fall from grace followed. AMR temporarily had lost three-quarters from the peak.

Shareholders have been burned as lower coal prices amid a challenging macro environment have led to significantly weaker operating results. AMR has been aggressively cutting costs and preserving cash in reaction. But after the strong bounce over the last months, I see the risks being tilted to the downside again. I expect the stock to fall back into the pit, rating it a sell, until shares trade below book for a reassessment.

Environment forces Coal on Ice

Alpha Metallurgical Resources just proved again with their Q3 2025 results that in met coal, you can't spell "profit" without pain right now.

Q3 shipments held steady at 3.9 million tons quarter over quarter but fell from 4.1 million tons year over year, as AMR has been reducing high-cost production to preserve margins and to avoid loss-making volumes.

Softer met coal indices dragged realized coking coal pricing down to $114.94/t (from $119.43/t in Q2).

As a result, adjusted EBITDA shrank roughly 20% year over year to $41.7 million. The company is posting operating and net losses while cash from operations is slowly melting, despite cost-cutting efforts, reaching $50.6 million in Q3, and not leaving much room for the resumption of the buyback program that was announced with Q2 2025 results after (reduced) CapEx.

Not everything is black, though. There have been a few positive aspects worth highlighting.

1. Costs are trending down. The operations team delivered $97.27/t in Q3—the lowest since 2021 and under the magical $100 mark. Productivity keeps climbing, strengthening the safety net in a challenging environment (more below).

2. Luckily, AMR's balance sheet remains free from dirt: $568.5 million of total liquidity, $408.5 million unrestricted cash, and in total net cash positive. That's a fifth of the current market cap in cash. Alpha is sitting on a cash mountain, offering financial independence and flexibility. Management is cautious with spending, and I like that. Better safe than sorry in this business.

However, we need to face the dusty reality. Global steel demand remains as exciting as reheated leftovers, steel prices hover around their 5-year lows, met/coking coal price indices are range-bound in the doldrums, and 2026 domestic contract talks with North American steelmakers are dragging on unusually long. Management openly warns: 2026 will likely be another "challenging" year.

Current met coal benchmark levels (17 November 2025):

  • Australian PLV: ~$196-198/t
  • US East Coast Low Vol: ~$177/t
  • US High Vol A: ~$150/t
  • US High Vol B: ~$140/t

Note: These prices differ from what companies like AMR report as realized prices. What Alpha (and every other coal company) actually pockets—their realized price—is much lower because the following costs are subtracted:

  • Train ride from mine to port
  • Terminal loading fees
  • Royalties to landowners & government
  • Federal black-lung tax + state severance taxes
  • Quality penalties or discounts

I wanted to remind readers that in 2024, we were already told that 2025 will see a rebound, which I cannot confirm has happened.

Management is communicating it openly, preparing shareholders for rough times in my view. To quote the CEO from the latest conference call (highlights mine):

Our focus remains on finishing the year strong by continuing to safely keep costs in line. Our cost discipline is especially important as we continue navigating the current stage of the market cycle and as metallurgical coal indexes reflect softness in the market environment. Broadly, the indexes [ hog on ] current levels for several months with oscillated pockets of volatility. The underlying economic conditions informing steel demand around the globe remain vulnerable to uncertainty and lackluster economic growth expectations. Against this backdrop, we're in the process of planning for 2026, putting together budgets and anticipating what we believe could be another challenging year for the coal industry.

While I am aware that there have been numerous more optimistic voices for long, pointing to pretend rising demand and undersupply, there are no signs in the real world for this. To the contrary, even AMR in their presentation is showing RISING supply over the next couple of years into an environment of fragile demand. There's nothing to sugarcoat.

Glancing over the valuation, we can see AMR is trading above its book value at 1.3x. Book value is a figure I like to use when assessing commodity stocks. Of course, it is not perfect, but it serves as a good and reliable yardstick. We can see the over-exaggeration towards the stock's peak. Since then, shares have traded on a seemingly low level.

But I'd like to caution readers and investors not to become too greedy. It is a good rule of thumb to fish for such stocks, preferably when they trade below their book value. Before the huge rally of 2021 and 2022 began, but also briefly this year, there were windows where AMR stock indeed traded with a decent margin of safety.

In light of little to no operational improvements and taking into account the current outlook of the management (despite no formal guidance at this stage), I see the stock as being priced too optimistically.

One or the other could point toward the announced buyback. AMR has almost $400 million of its $1.5 billion repurchase program outstanding. But the pace is more of a cosmetic nature, as FCF does not allow for more, and I do not expect management to sacrifice their liquidity buffer.

Risks to My Thesis

If the supply and demand imbalance switches back to lower supply and higher demand, met coal prices would likely rise, pulling up stocks like AMR.

Accidents and natural higher forces could constrain supply temporarily, benefitting AMR if they are not hit.

The sector could consolidate further, as the case of Arch Resources and Consol has shown. In this case, a takeover bid with a premium would invalidate my price target.

Conclusion

AMR is sitting on a comfortable war chest and making progress on operational efficiency. However, with the lack of positive catalysts and cautious management for next year, I rate the stock a sell, as I see no reason why it should trade for 1.3x book value. I expect shares to fall back to their lows from earlier this year, which, to give a round number, would be $100.

data.title: The Fed Will Cut Rates In December Supporting Year-End Rally
data.description:
Stocks struggled again yesterday, led by the largest technology companies in what is now the longest slide for the indexes since August. The silver lining is that sector rotation is afoot, and the Russell 2000 index of small-cap stocks was higher throughout the day to close with a small gain. That tells me that investors are starting to look for bargains in names that have been unduly punished. I also think it suggests that the Fed will cut rates in December, contrary to the consensus view. I still view this as nothing more than a much needed pullback, which is closer to being over than just getting started. Therefore, I am looking for bargains as well.

As the government’s economic reports start to roll out, beginning with Thursday’s employment data, I expect the probability of a rate cut on December 10 will increase well above 50% as the meeting approaches. The private sector reports from ADP and Challenger indicate that the labor market continues to stagnate, which I think will tip the scales in favor of another rate reduction. That should put an end to this pullback, which may end up being a correction for the tech-heavy Nasdaq. Regardless, we still have a very strong backdrop for the market with narrow credit spreads, record profit margins, the government reopening, and breadth in earnings growth for the Russell 3000 strengthening. It has just been overshadowed by the myopic focus on AI-related investments where stock prices were running well ahead of fundamentals.

I have been sharing numerous precedents in quantitative form over the past several months for an important reason — they often defy logic and bearish sentiment but tend to do a much better job of predicting the future. The S&P 500 rose 17% through the first ten months of this year. Since 1950, returns that exceeded 10% during the first ten months resulted in an average gain of 7.3% in the November and December that followed. Perhaps this time is different, but this lines up with my fundamental outlook and expectation for a year-end rally.

According to Bespoke Investment Group, the percentage of stocks in the S&P 500 index that are oversold (44%) have hit its highest level since the correction last April, while the percentage that is overbought has fallen to just 16%.

The largest company in the world (Nvidia) may join the oversold group before this corrective phase is over, as the shares have traded lower on the day of its earnings reports more often than not over the past two years. Yet the stock has corrected 14% from its all-time high heading into this report, which leaves it trading at a more reasonable 26 times next year’s consensus estimate. A beat on the top and bottom lines along with an increase in guidance will likely support the share price.

If it is followed by another soft labor report before tomorrow’s open, this period of consolidation for the market may be over. Still, I am looking for the rotation we have seen from the hyper-growth momentum stocks to more value-oriented sectors and names to continue. What has led since the April low is not likely to lead moving forward.

data.title: VGMS: Cross-Sectional Multi Sector ETF
data.description:
Thesis

The Vanguard Multi-Sector Income Bond ETF (VGMS) is a new addition to the fixed income universe having come to market in June 2025. The fund is part of the Vanguard family, and represents a cross-sectional take on fixed income markets:

This actively managed fixed income fund seeks to provide broadly diversified exposure across bonds of any credit quality. The low-cost fund invests in U.S. Treasury, corporate bonds, and emerging-market bonds. Using a disciplined, risk-controlled approach, the fund seeks to outperform its benchmark through security selection, sector allocation, and duration decisions.

As we shall see from the below sections, the ETF has roughly 50% of its holdings in high yield, thus providing a balanced approach to fixed income.

In this article we are going to have a closer look at the fund, its current composition, and its outlook in today's macro environment.

Current composition for this active ETF

Let us make sure readers understand VGMS is an active ETF, meaning the holdings can change substantially from month to month. As of now, the fund holds the following sectors:

  • ABS securities: 8.3%
  • Corporate Financial: 17.2%
  • Corporate Industrial: 53%
  • CMBS: 1.2%
  • Treasuries: 3.8%
  • Foreign Bonds (including EM): 10.5%
  • Corporate Utilities: 3.6%
  • Other: residual

Currently the fund is overweight corporate paper via its composition. From a ratings perspective the holdings are a cross-sectional take on credit:

  • AAA: 3%
  • AA: 1.5%
  • A: 12%
  • BBB: 25%
  • BB: 31%
  • B: 21%
  • CCC: 3%
  • Treasuries: 3.8%

As we can see from the ratings distribution above, the fund is distributed across ratings buckets and spectrum, with roughly 55% of the holdings in the below investment grade bucket.

The ETF represents a larger take on the fixed income markets, but unlike some of the aggregate funds the name focuses on credit risky instruments. Other analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.128 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.3%
  • Duration: 3.6 years
  • Portfolio YTM: 5.8%

Although the data is very limited so far (i.e. we do not have one year of data), the volatility metrics are very encouraging, with a 2.7% volatility level and a Sharpe ratio of 1.9. The true test for this fund will lie with an actual recession, and its drawdown versus a balanced HY and IG portfolio. We expect the name to do well due to its short duration and active management.

Performance so far

Since the ETF is new, we do not have much in terms of data, but let us see how the fund has done:

We are comparing the ETF with the iShares iBoxx Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) and the PIMCO Multisector Bond ETF (PYLD). PYLD is an active fund, while JNK and LQD are passive ETFs. PYLD is the outperformer, closely followed by VGMS. In today's macro environment active ETFs tend to do much better due to the ability of managers to pivot towards undervalued sectors of the market.

We are also using LQD and JNK here since theoretically an investor can try to replicate VGMS by using a 50% JNK, 50% LQD allocation (a very imperfect replication but one that can be seen as such).

Macro picture - tight spreads

From a macro perspective spreads are very tight historically:

The blue line above represents HY corporate spreads, and as we can see from the graph they are currently near the bottom of their historic range. During risk-off events HY spreads trade closer to 6%, while during times like today they are at 3%. While we do not know how long they can stay low, technically speaking the only way is up from here.

Similarly, the orange line above represents BBB spreads, and they too are very low. While IG spreads are not as volatile as HY, we can clearly see the same trend as for HY spreads. We do not particularly like spreads here, but an active ETF can pick and choose very robust credits, all while keeping duration very low.

As a take-away from this section, the macro is not very good for fixed income in general, with spreads at historic tights.

Best approach? DCA

In our view, the best approach for VGMS is a dollar cost averaging one. We like the fund, its active approach and performance so far, but we hate spreads here. Therefore we are of the opinion the best approach for this ETF is to pursue a 12-month DCA strategy. As we saw earlier in the year, specifically April 2025, we can get bouts of risk-off when credit sells off. Via a DCA approach entry points are smoothed out so that the average entry point is an appealing one. We are therefore slapping a 'Buy' rating on this name but with a DCA strategy in mind.

Conclusion

VGMS is a fixed income exchange traded fund. The name contains a blend of high yield and investment grade names, but more importantly is an active fund. Active ETFs are ideal for today's markets where spreads are very tight. An active manager can continuously adjust a portfolio, and position it to go into undervalued sectors. We like VGMS and its strategy, and are of the belief that a DCA buying strategy is best suited for this name in the current macro environment.

data.title: Intellia Therapeutics: A Worst-Case Risk Becomes A Tragic Reality (Downgrade)
data.description:
It happened!

I’m referring to a risk factor — the worst possible risk factor — coming to pass.

Biotech and pharmaceuticals are risky businesses. We know that.

These companies spend a fortune to develop medical treatments. Many flop. Often it happens in the lab — before they’re tried on humans. Many others fail in trial. Some don’t work on humans. Some work, but no better than other already-available treatments. Others show promise but falter due to unacceptable side effects.

And these risks come on top of the usual assortment of business risks. How many patients are afflicted with the relevant condition? Will physicians prescribe it? Can patients afford it? Will insurance cover it? How does it compare to competitive treatments? For early-stage R&D-oriented firms, can they survive long enough to make money?

We in the investment community understand and have become accustomed to coping with such risks.

We diversify beyond biotech-pharma. We manage position sizes so no single stock will kill our portfolios. We diversify within the field by owing several such stocks. We really, really diversify within the field by owning specialty ETFs such as the State Street SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI) or, higher on the risk scale, something like the Virtus LifeSci Clinical Trials ETF (BBC).

Sometimes, though, and fortunately very rarely, the ultimate disaster happens. I’m referring a participant in a drug trial losing his life.

Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ: NTLA) recently got hit with…

A Worst-Case Outcome

A participant in one of its trials passed away.

We do not now know, and we certainly cannot now say that Nex-z, the treatment NTLA was testing, caused his death.

It’s still early days. At present, the focus remains on fact-finding.

Not surprisingly, analysts vigorously questioned CEO John Leonard about causation during a call held on 10/27/25 to initially discuss the tragedy, and again during an earnings call on 11/7/25.

Equally unsurprising, Leonard could not on either occasion supply definitive answers. I don’t blame him for that. It’s still too early.

We cannot expect any responsible CEO to speak definitively given the currently-skimpy state of available knowledge. Doing so would have been reckless. And Leonard is to be applauded for refusing to go that route. He’ll speak further when more information becomes available. That’s the proper stance.

Wall Street traders and investors play by different rules. They are allowed to act as if the trial caused the death and as if NTLA will be seriously damaged. The Street doesn’t need facts. All it needs is aversion to uncertainty. And with NTLA right now, it has plenty of that.

Still, let’s try to make as much sense of the situation as we can.

What We Know

(1) Key Terminology — Jargon

If you invest in biotech (or pharma), it helps a lot if you really love jargon. If not, you should cultivate at least a waking tolerance of it.

Here are key terms you’ll see throughout NTLA communications and discussions of same.

  • Nex-z (formerly known as NTLA-2001) - This is a gene-editing protocol to address a condition known as ATTR.
  • ATTR (transthyretin amyloidosis) - This is a very serious condition involving the liver. A gene improperly folds. That causes blockages. I’m sure you know, through your own medical situation, that “blockage” is a word you never want a physician to say to you. It’s bad. It can be fatal.
  • ATTR-CM (ATTR with cardiomyopathy) - ATTR accompanied by cardiac-related problems.
  • MAGNITUDE - This is the name of a late-stage Phase III clinical trial involving about 650 persons suffering from ATTR-CM.
  • ATTR-PN (ATTR with polyneuropathy) - ATTR accompanied by nerve-related problems.
  • MAGNITUDE 2 - This is the name of a late-stage Phase III clinical trial involving about 47 participants suffering from ATTR-PN.
  • Ionvo-z (formerly known as NTLA-2002) - This is a gene-editing protocol to address a condition known as HAE.
  • HAE (hereditary angioedema) - This is genetic defect that causes painful swelling in various parts of the body.
  • HAELO - This is the name of a late-stage clinical trial involving about 60 people suffering from HAE.

(2) Our Focus

The relevant issue today concerns testing of Nex-z for patients with ATTR-CM.

This is where participants experienced noteworthy adverse events.

No such events have been reported for patients with ATTR-PN. But as I’ll discuss below, fallout from the ATTR-CM MAGNITUDE study will impact progress in ATTR-PN MAGNITUDE 2 progress.

We are not now concerned with Ionvo-z or HAELO. All there is proceeding on schedule.

All HAELO participants NTLA planned to recruit have by now been enrolled. On the 11/7/25 call, NTLA reported that it is…

on track to share top line data by mid-2026, submit a BLA to the FDA in the second half of 2026, and prepare for an anticipated commercial launch in the U.S. in the first half of 2027.

(3) Chronology— Prelude

I’m going to start by backtracking a bit.

The Street has, for a while, had an antsy love-hate relationship with NTLA.

Back on 10/30/24, I discussed investors having turned against the company based on test data that the Street thought was bad but which the FDA deemed fine.

A bit later, NTLA decided to abandon a project it felt was going nowhere. It chose, instead, to cut costs and confine resources to promising ventures (Nex-Z and Ionvo-z). Considering that pre-commercial biotechs like this should be conserving cash, I thought this was a sensible idea. But as I discussed in my 1/14/25 report, the Street freaked out.

Then, in my 7/18/25 NTLA write-up, I discussed a 5/28/25 adverse event.

An ATTR-CM MAGNITUDE participant was shown to have some abnormally high liver-enzyme tallies. It was asymptomatic — it was only discovered in a routine study-related test.

Had that situation persisted, it would have been dangerous. But that didn’t happen. The situation seems to have resolved on its own without hospitalization or medical action.

The stock fell hard when the issue was first disclosed. But it recovered, and then some, after everything resolved. My 7/18/25 article discussed it further.

NTLA stock thereafter rallied briskly. But let’s not forget the Street’s recent angst. We might assume many recent buyers had, essentially, been dragged kicking and screaming into buying.

So it’s natural to respond to the tragedy at hand by saying, even if not aloud, “Uh oh, here we go again.”

I presented this Prelude to address readers who may understandably think or feel this way. Know, however, that…

Notwithstanding what some Wall Street analysts previously assumed about trial data, Phase I and II test results were good enough for the FDA to allow Nex-z to move on to Phase III. Historically, about 33% of drugs that pass Phases I and II get to move on the Phase III.

Also, note that medically speaking, the latest event has nothing at all to do with the previous adverse event. The latter participant was asymptomatic and his challenging enzme levels resolved spontaneously without hospitalization.

(4) Chronology — The Current Tragedy

On 10/27/25…

NTLA learned that a MAGNITUDE study participant, who had last been dosed on 9/30/24, entered a hospital. That happened after he told his study principal investigator about abdominal pain.

It turned out that two key liver enzymes exceeded the upper limits of normal ranges; one threefold and another fourfold.

Based on the MAGNITUDE protocol, NTLA immediately ceased dosing any patient with Nex-z.

Although not required by the MAGNITUDE-2 protocol, NTLA also chose to cease dosing ATTR-PN patients in that study.

Also, NTLA quickly started…

consulting with clinical investigators and external experts to ensure optimal care for the patient and further assess the event itself. We've now mandated clinical sites to collect additional labs from patients in the initial weeks following dosing to detect potential liver elevation sooner.

Additionally, the company also started consulting with “other potential monitoring and risk mitigation strategies…. (and) global regulatory authorities” to consider other precautionary measures.

Capping it off, CEO John Leonard stated during the 11/7/25 call, that…

we've taken a number of actions in the interest of patient safety. For instance, we've mandated that clinical sites collect additional labs from patients in the initial weeks following dosing to detect potential liver elevation sooner. An internal team has been closely reviewing the blinded safety data, baseline characteristics, among other factors, to identify potential contributors to the liver-related events seen in MAGNITUDE.

On 10/29/25…

The FDA placed formal holds on further MAGNITUDE and MAGNITUDE 2 testing.

On 11/6/25…

The hospitalized participant passed away.

(5) The Cause of Death

With respect to the 80-something participant, Leonard stated on the 11/7/25 call that NTLA had been...

advised by the treating physician that this is a case with complicating comorbidities, and the case is being further evaluated.

As mentioned above, analysts had many follow-up questions. But on the call and to date, Leonard has been and still is unable to provide further answer.

This is frustrating.

But it’s also understandable.

We’re all accustomed to news, fictional and real, in which coroners are able to quickly determine causes of death. Sometimes, though, reality does not cooperate easily with investigators.

The interrelationships between what we know (advanced age, administration of Nex-z, and “complicating comorbidities”) will take time to sort out.

Uncertainty averse as it may be, the Street has no choice but to be patient and await the development of further facts.

Looking Ahead

Besides cause of death, another unknown with which we all must cope is the future of Nex-z.

At this time, Leonard is, absolutely positively not throwing in the towel on this project:

First, ATTR amyloidosis is a disease with high mortality. While undeniable progress has been made in this treatment, current therapies only slow its advance and most patients continue to face progressive morbidity and mortality. Second, we've enrolled more than 650 patients in MAGNITUDE and 47 patients in MAGNITUDE-2. To date, Grade 4 liver transaminase elevations have been reported in less than 1% of all patients enrolled in MAGNITUDE. Each of these cases has been observed approximately 3 to 5 weeks following randomization and dosing. There have been no Grade 4 liver transaminase elevations in MAGNITUDE-2. And third, we are highly encouraged by the data from our ongoing Phase I clinical trial of nex-z. On Monday, in a late-breaker oral session at the 2025 AHA Scientific Sessions in New Orleans, we will have the opportunity to share longer-term data for nexz that we believe demonstrates its potential to improve various disease measures and mortality.

Obviously, though, Leonard is pulling the plug on the timetable for expected progress on Nex-z:

Given the clinical hold, we are unable to maintain our milestone guidance for nex-z, and we expect to provide an update once we finalize the plan with regulators.

As noted above, everything related to Ionvo-z and HAELO is proceeding as previously planned.

But given NTLA’s pre-commercial status and the hold on Nex-Z testing, we have to wonder about financial survivability.

There’s no immediate danger. According to CFO Edward Dulac on the 11/7/25 earnings call:

Our cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities were $669.9 million as of September 30, 2025, compared to $861.7 million as of December 31, 2024. During the third quarter, we raised approximately $115 million from our ATM facility. When combined with the benefits of the restructuring initiatives we implemented in early 2025, this enables us to extend our cash runway into mid-2027 and through lonvo-z's anticipated commercial launch in the U.S. for HAE.

That’s not completely comforting. The currently-uncertain future for Nex-z still makes for a dark cloud.

But Dulac’s remarks suggest NTLA is not now walking along the edge of a cliff on a path covered with banana peels.

Risk

This is obvious The risk involves all things relating to Nex-z.

What to do About NTLA Stock

As a pre-commercial company, valuation ratios have always been inappropriate for NTLA. And now with the ongoing Nex-z crisis, the day when valuation metrics become relevant is being pushed further into the future.

Here’s NTLA’s price chart.

That looks terrible.

The recently strong 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) made an abrupt news-driven U-turn and crashed through the 50-day EMA.

We saw likewise with Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). (Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.)

In my most recent 7/18/25 report, I said “Buy.”

Given the events discussed here, it should come as no surprise that this rating aged horribly. Since then, NTLA is down 30%, versus a 5% S&P 500 gain.

But that’s not the least of it. The stock is now down about 70% from its pre-tragedy peak.

Nobody wants to buy high and sell low. So it would obviously hurt badly to cut and run now. But if one must, then one should.

That said, recognizing our inability to roll the clock back on the post-tragedy pain, and the reality that the current stock price now discounts a lot of bad news, I don’t believe it’s necessary at this time to say “Sell” (which, in my taxonomy, means underperform).

While it is possible that further investigation might reveal an unsolvable problem with Nex-z, the facts now known suggest we need not run to that extreme.

Without making light of a participant death, it’s one person out of the many who’ve received Nex-z. And we have been told that other factors may have contributed to or outright caused the outcome.

Also, we see NTLA working to discover protocols it hopes will pre-identify at-risk persons. That would ultimately put Nex-z on par with many other medications…

Physicians and pharmacists continually stay aware of recognized risk factors and steer at-risk patients away from that which could harm them. (Speaking for myself, GLP-1 drugs could help me a lot, but I’m compelled to avoid them due to high risk, for me, of pancreatic cancer.) So even if we learn that Nex-z is dangerous to some people, we should not assume it will vanish as a failure.

But not saying “Sell” doesn’t mean I can stand pat at a Buy rating (which in my taxonomy, translates to outperform).

Given the MAGNITUDE and MAGNITUDE 2 testing halts, our inability now to guess how long that will last, the worst-case scenario still lurking in the background, and the Street’s habitual angst regarding NTLA (as described above in the chronology Prologue), I find it hard to envision NTLA outperforming the market any time soon.

So for now, and probably for a while, I balance all that by rating NTLA as a neutral relative (to the market) performer. I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by classifying NTLA as a Hold.
